name: RustPython
description: Python 3 interpreter written entirely in Rust, aiming for CPython 3.12+ compatibility

license.comment: MIT license for code, CC-BY-4.0 for logo

implementation.comment: Pure Rust implementation, no CPython bindings. Optional C dependencies only for SSL.

documentation.comment: rustpython-vm is the main embedding crate

latest-version.comment: Released Aug 2024. Weekly pre-releases available on GitHub.

typing.comment: Python's dynamic duck typing; optional type hints available but not enforced at runtime

closures.comment: Python supports closures via nested functions

iterators-generators.comment: Python has iterator protocol and generator functions

module-system.comment: Python has a comprehensive import/module system

standard-library.comment: Aims to implement full Python stdlib; pip works with SSL feature

execution-strategy.comment: Compiles to bytecode; experimental JIT available via 'jit' feature flag

compilation-model.comment: Runtime eval and can serialize bytecode (.pyc files)

debugging-support.comment: Python has pdb debugger; integration level varies

source-maps.comment: Python tracebacks include file and line information

startup-time.comment: Larger runtime than minimal interpreters; initialization overhead

execution-speed.comment: Bytecode interpreter; comparable to CPython in many benchmarks, JIT experimental

memory-overhead.comment: Full Python runtime requires moderate memory; ~115K lines of code

garbage-collection.comment: Uses reference counting like CPython, with cycle detection

pure-rust.comment: Core is pure Rust; optional C deps only for SSL features

api-safety.comment: Unsafe contained internally; public API uses safe Rust patterns

idiomatic-api.comment: Macro-based registration (#[pyfunction], #[pymodule]); 12.67% doc coverage indicates gaps

serde-integration.comment: Optional serde support via feature flag

async-compatible.comment: Python async is internal to interpreter; Rust integration is synchronous

type-mapping.comment: TryFromObject/PyPayload traits provide automatic mapping; manual conversion also possible

wasm-compatible.comment: Compiles to WebAssembly WASI; online demo available

blank-slate-mode.comment: Interpreter::without_stdlib() creates interpreter with no built-ins

memory-sandboxing.comment: Scripts cannot access arbitrary host memory

cpu-limits.comment: No built-in instruction count or execution time limits

syscall-restrictions.comment: Using without_stdlib removes file/network access; host controls what's available

capability-security.comment: No fine-grained capability system; all-or-nothing stdlib inclusion

default-deny.comment: Without stdlib, scripts have no I/O capabilities by default

call-script-from-rust.comment: vm.run_code_obj() and similar APIs for calling Python from Rust

share-data.comment: PyRef types allow sharing references; primitives are cloned

custom-types.comment: #[pyclass] and PyPayload derive for exposing Rust types

operator-overloading.comment: Python's __add__, __mul__, etc. protocols supported for custom types

method-syntax.comment: #[pymethod] attribute for defining methods on custom types

callbacks-events.comment: Python functions can be stored and called as callbacks

last-release.comment: v0.4.0; weekly pre-releases continue on GitHub

package-manager.comment: pip works with SSL feature enabled

editor-support.comment: Uses standard Python syntax; existing Python tooling (pylsp, black, etc.) works

example-projects.comment: GreptimeDB uses RustPython for embedded scripting

documentation-quality.comment: 12.67% docs.rs coverage; basic examples available but limited tutorials

maturity.comment: Explicitly states 'not production ready'; usable for WASM and embedding experiments

primary-use-cases.comment: Best for Python scripting in Rust apps; GreptimeDB time-series DB uses it
